expected_findings.summary: Passive K-1 loss from Synthetic Partners 39 LP must be suspended
expected_findings.detail: The K-1 is marked passive and has a loss. Per IRC §469 passive losses can only offset passive income. If there is no passive income to absorb the loss this year, it must be suspended on Form 8582 and carried forward.

expected_findings.summary: PTET election can effectively uncap the SALT deduction on K-1 income
expected_findings.detail: IRS Notice 2020-75 blessed state-level PTET regimes as a way to pay state tax at the entity level, converting a capped SALT deduction into a federal ordinary business expense that passes through as lower K-1 income. Consider the PTET election with each state where the K-1 generates income.

expected_findings.summary: Quinn has up to $7,000 of TY2025 IRA headroom
expected_findings.detail: Quinn is 41 years old; the TY2025 IRA contribution limit is $7,000. Deductibility may phase out at high AGI for traditional IRA if covered by an employer plan; Roth IRA phases out at $236K–$246K MFJ.

expected_findings.summary: AGI above Roth IRA phaseout — consider backdoor Roth
expected_findings.detail: AGI $419,900 is above the single Roth IRA phaseout ceiling ($165,000). Direct Roth contributions are not allowed. Consider the backdoor Roth strategy (nondeductible traditional IRA contribution + Roth conversion) if there is no pre-tax IRA balance to trigger pro-rata.
Quinn has contributed $14,000 toward the TY2025 §402(g) limit of $23,500. Another $9,500 can still be deferred before year end.

expected_findings.summary: California PTET election available on pass-through income
expected_findings.detail: California allows a pass-through entity to elect to pay state tax at the entity level, producing a federal ordinary business deduction that effectively uncaps the SALT cost on this K-1. The return has eligible pass-through income but the state slice is not flagged as having made the election.

expected_findings.summary: Pass-through income allocated across 2+ PTET states
expected_findings.detail: Multiple states allow a PTET election but apportion the deduction differently. Consult the partnership's state apportionment schedule before electing — the federal benefit stacks per state.

expected_findings.summary: Large SALT deduction — run Form 6251 to check AMT exposure
expected_findings.detail: Total state + property tax is $25,200. SALT is added back for AMT under §56(b)(1), which can tip a high-income household into AMT even at TY2025 exemption levels.

expected_findings.summary: Need $68,024 withheld to hit 90% current year safe harbor
expected_findings.detail: The alternative safe harbor is 90% of current year tax. This is usually only easier to hit than 110% prior year if income dropped.
